Internal link to  conversation – [https://chatgpt.com/c/698d117e-9c38-832a-8080-5baa5d9d9f44](https://chatgpt.com/c/698d117e-9c38-832a-8080-5baa5d9d9f44)  and maybe [https://chatgpt.com/c/698517ae-41e4-832d-b95b-0b22e98e26e3](https://chatgpt.com/c/698517ae-41e4-832d-b95b-0b22e98e26e3) 

*Prompt to GPT 5.2 pro* 

*Consider the comments above and attached as well as the slightly updated model pasted below . Propose, build, and explain a model incorporating the suggested improvements. It should also incorporate the values and responses elicited in our specific 'pivotal questions' (attached) in some way. I want to provide a flexible example to our CM PQ evaluators. .. Also a template for them to buld/consider their own model. (It could be in Squiggle hub, or feel free to suggest something different). And maybe something intermediat e-- a given structure, but where you can choose a range of assumptions, distributions, and parameters, as inputs (If it works better \-- you can provide instructions to claude code to build the interface mentioned) Finally, build another model (with Squiggle implementation if possible, or Python if you need the flexibility), starting from first-principles, informed by the research and evidence, but not limited by previous assumption, structure, and path-dependence.* 

Below is a **proposed \+ built** upgrade path that:

* **Fixes the biggest structural issues** in the current Squiggle model (esp. the “sample() toggles” problem, hidden scale/utilization, and bounded distributions for probabilities/shares).  
    
* **Makes it easy for PQ evaluators** to plug in their own elicited ranges/distributions.  
    
* Provides:  
    
  1. an **improved, PQ‑integrated Squiggle model** (paste into SquiggleHub), and  
  2. a **separate first‑principles “mechanistic TEA skeleton”** (Python), which is more flexible and closer to engineering structure.

A quick “why these changes” note first:

* Your current model uses `sample(Dist.bernoulli(...))` for the tech switches (hydrolysates, food‑grade micros). In Squiggle, `sample(dist)` returns **one number** (a single draw), not a distribution. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/Dist)) That means **within any given run**, the model is essentially “locked” into one scenario (hydro yes/no), and the output distribution mostly reflects only the *other* uncertainties—**not** uncertainty over which tech regime you end up in. This lines up with the critique that scenario mixing becomes messy / non‑comparable across runs.  
    
* Also, your `0.4 to 0.7` syntax is a shorthand for a **lognormal** distribution with p5/p95, which is awkward for probabilities/shares because it can exceed 1\. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/Dist)) Better: **Beta** (bounded) or **Uniform**.  
    
* The “hidden scale” complaint is real in your v0.1: you define CAPEX as `$ / annual kg capacity` and fixed OPEX as `$ / kg`, but you never explicitly define **capacity, utilization, or annual throughput**, so the “per kg” numbers implicitly embed those. This is exactly the “implicit scale” point noted in your eval doc. 【155:0†Cultivated Meat Model Eval.docx†L35-L105】

---

# **Model 1: PQ‑integrated improved Squiggle model (paste ite for evaluators\*\*. It keeps the simple accounting identity, but:**

* makes **scale \+ uptime explicit**  
* uses **bounded distributions** for probabilities/shares  
* fixes scenario toggles so they’re **properly propagated across Monte Carlo samples** (not “one draw per run”)  
* introduces an **optional maturity factor** to create realistic positive correlations  
* splits “micronutrients” into **commodity micros** vs **recombinant factors** (a common critique)

Key technical trick: instead of `sample(bernoulli(...))`, we create a **Bernoulli distribution** (sample set) and then use `SampleSet.map` to draw from the correct conditional distributions *per sample*. `SampleSet.map` is intended for exactly this kind of sample‑wise transformation. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/SampleSet))

---

```
/*
Cultivated Meat Unit Cost Model (PQ-integrated) — v0.2 template
Purpose: Give CM PQ evaluators a transparent, editable template.

Major upgrades vs v0.1:
1) Avoid "sample() toggles" that lock the whole run into one scenario.
   Instead: bernoulli(p) is a distribution, then SampleSet.map applies scenario logic per sample.
2) Explicit scale + uptime so $/kg quantities are not hiding plant size/utilization.
3) Use bounded distributions (beta/uniform) for probabilities/shares instead of `a to b` (lognormal) for [0,1] vars.
4) Optional maturity factor to induce correlation between "worlds" (tech adoption, capex, WACC).
5) Split micros into commodity micros vs recombinant factors (growth factors, albumin-like proteins).
*/

/// === Helpers ===
/// Convert kTA to kg/year nameplate (1 kTA = 1,000 metric tons/year = 1e6 kg/year)
ktaToKgPerYear(kta) = kta * 1e6

/// Draw from one of two distributions, controlled by a Bernoulli SampleSetDist (0/1),
/// returning a SampleSetDist of numbers.
drawByToggle(toggle01, distIf1, distIf0) =
  SampleSet.map(
    SampleSet.fromDist(toggle01),
    {|t| if t == 1 then sample(distIf1) else sample(distIf0)}
  )

/// === Pivotal-question inputs (edit these first) ===
/// Think of these as "priors" elicited from PQ evaluators.
/// Use beta() for probabilities/shares and lognormal()/uniform() for positive variables.

@name("Maturity index (0-1)")
@doc("Latent 'industry maturity' factor to correlate adoption, costs, and financing. Optional but helpful.")
maturity = beta({mean: 0.5, stdev: 0.2})

@name("Plant nameplate capacity (kTA/yr)")
@doc("Explicit scale. Replace with PQ-elicited distribution or set pointMass(20) to mimic Risner 20 kTA.")
plant_kta = lognormal({p5: 5, p95: 50})

@name("Uptime / utilization (0-1)")
@doc("Fraction of nameplate capacity actually produced (uptime, utilization).")
uptime = beta({mean: 0.9, stdev: 0.05})

@name("Base prob hydrolysates widely used by 2036 (0-1)")
p_hydro_base = beta({mean: 0.6, stdev: 0.1})

@name("Base prob food-grade commodity micros by 2036 (0-1)")
p_foodgrade_base = beta({mean: 0.65, stdev: 0.1})

@name("Base prob cheap recombinant factors by 2036 (0-1)")
@doc("Captures progress on growth factors / recombinant proteins cost reduction.")
p_recfactors_base = beta({mean: 0.5, stdev: 0.15})

/// Optional: correlate adoption probabilities with maturity
@name("Hydrolysates adoption probability (maturity-adjusted)")
p_hydro = truncate(p_hydro_base + 0.25 * (maturity - 0.5), 0, 1)

@name("Food-grade micros probability (maturity-adjusted)")
p_foodgrade = truncate(p_foodgrade_base + 0.20 * (maturity - 0.5), 0, 1)

@name("Cheap recombinant factors probability (maturity-adjusted)")
p_recfactors = truncate(p_recfactors_base + 0.25 * (maturity - 0.5), 0, 1)

/// Scenario toggles as *distributions* (not single samples!)
is_hydro = bernoulli(p_hydro)
is_foodgrade = bernoulli(p_foodgrade)
is_recfactors_cheap = bernoulli(p_recfactors)

/// === Process intensities & prices (conditional on scenario) ===
/// Bulk media (macros)
l_media_per_kg = drawByToggle(is_hydro, 5 to 20, 15 to 60)
price_media_per_l = drawByToggle(is_hydro, 0.2 to 1.2, 1 to 4)

/// Commodity micronutrients (vitamins/minerals/salts etc)
g_comm_micros_per_kg = drawByToggle(is_foodgrade, 0.1 to 2, 1 to 10)
price_comm_micros_per_g = drawByToggle(is_foodgrade, 0.02 to 2, 0.5 to 20)

/// Recombinant factors (growth factors / albumin-like proteins)
/// (Kept extremely flexible; evaluators should replace with their elicited ranges)
g_recfactors_per_kg = drawByToggle(is_recfactors_cheap, 0.0001 to 0.01, 0.001 to 0.2)
price_recfactors_per_g = drawByToggle(is_recfactors_cheap, 0.05 to 20, 5 to 500)

/// === Variable cost blocks ===
voc_media = l_media_per_kg * price_media_per_l
voc_comm_micros = g_comm_micros_per_kg * price_comm_micros_per_g
voc_recfactors = g_recfactors_per_kg * price_recfactors_per_g

@name("Other variable costs ($/kg)")
@doc("Utilities, disposables, waste, packaging. (Avoid double-counting fixed labor here.)")
voc_other = 0.5 to 5

voc_total = voc_media + voc_comm_micros + voc_recfactors + voc_other

/// === Capital + financing (explicit scale + uptime) ===
@name("WACC (maturity-adjusted)")
wacc_base = lognormal({p5: 0.08, p95: 0.20})
wacc = truncateLeft(wacc_base - 0.03 * (maturity - 0.5), 0.03)

@name("Asset lifetime (years)")
asset_life = uniform(8, 20)

crf = wacc * (1 + wacc) ^ asset_life / ((1 + wacc) ^ asset_life - 1)

/// CAPEX model: set at reference scale then apply economy-of-scale exponent
ref_kta = 20
scale_ratio = plant_kta / ref_kta

@name("Total CAPEX scaling exponent (K ~ Q^s)")
@doc("If s<1, capex per unit capacity falls with scale. Typical TEA assumes ~0.6-0.9.")
capex_scale_exp = uniform(0.6, 0.9)

@name("Pharma-grade CAPEX @ 20kTA ($ per annual kg capacity)")
capex_pharma_ref_percap = 5 to 25

capex_pharma_percap =
  capex_pharma_ref_percap * (scale_ratio ^ (capex_scale_exp - 1))

@name("Custom reactor share (0-1)")
custom_reactor_share_base = beta({mean: 0.55, stdev: 0.15})
custom_reactor_share = truncate(custom_reactor_share_base + 0.30*(maturity-0.5), 0, 1)

@name("Custom vs pharma CAPEX ratio")
custom_capex_ratio = uniform(0.35, 0.85)

capex_avg_percap =
  capex_pharma_percap *
  (custom_reactor_share * custom_capex_ratio + (1 - custom_reactor_share))

/// Divide by uptime to convert $/kg-capacity to $/kg-produced
capital_cost_perkg = capex_avg_percap * crf / uptime

/// === Fixed OPEX (explicit scale + uptime) ===
/// Define fixed OPEX at ref scale via a per-kg reference, then scale and divide by output
@name("Fixed OPEX per kg @ 20kTA and 0.9 uptime ($/kg)")
fixed_opex_perkg_ref = uniform(1, 6)

ref_output_kgpy = ktaToKgPerYear(ref_kta) * 0.9
fixed_opex_annual_ref = fixed_opex_perkg_ref * ref_output_kgpy

@name("Fixed OPEX scaling exponent (F ~ Q^s)")
fixed_opex_scale_exp = uniform(0.6, 1.0)

fixed_opex_annual = fixed_opex_annual_ref * (scale_ratio ^ fixed_opex_scale_exp)

output_kgpy = ktaToKgPerYear(plant_kta) * uptime
fixed_opex_perkg = fixed_opex_annual / output_kgpy

/// === Total fully-loaded unit cost ===
/// Note: This includes a capital charge (WACC/CRF), so it's closer to a "levelized unit cost"
/// than accounting COGS.
unit_cost = voc_total + capital_cost_perkg + fixed_opex_perkg

/// === Diagnostics: compute the "hard-coded" % reductions as *implied* by your parameterization ===
media_cost_nohydro = (15 to 60) * (1 to 4)
media_cost_hydro = (5 to 20) * (0.2 to 1.2)
implied_media_reduction = 1 - median(media_cost_hydro) / median(media_cost_nohydro)

comm_micros_cost_nofg = (1 to 10) * (0.5 to 20)
comm_micros_cost_fg = (0.1 to 2) * (0.02 to 2)
implied_comm_micros_reduction = 1 - median(comm_micros_cost_fg) / median(comm_micros_cost_nofg)

/// === Outputs ===
summary = {
  unit_p5 = quantile(unit_cost, 0.05)
  unit_p50 = quantile(unit_cost, 0.50)
  unit_p95 = quantile(unit_cost, 0.95)
  unit_mean = mean(unit_cost)

  prob_lt_10 = cdf(unit_cost, 10)
  prob_lt_25 = cdf(unit_cost, 25)

  media_share_voc = median(voc_media / voc_total)
  comm_micros_share_voc = median(voc_comm_micros / voc_total)
  recfactors_share_voc = median(voc_recfactors / voc_total)
  capex_share_total = median(capital_cost_perkg / unit_cost)

  { unit_p5, unit_p50, unit_p95, unit_mean, prob_lt_10, prob_lt_25,
    media_share_voc, comm_micros_share_voc, recfactors_share_voc, capex_share_total,
    implied_media_reduction, implied_comm_micros_reduction
  }
}
```

---

## **What this fixes (mapped to your critique points)**

### **1\) “Scenario toggles / Bernoulli sampling is intransparent…”**

Your v0.1 literally does: `is_hydro = sample(Dist.bernoulli(median(p_hydrolysates)))`【161:0†squiggle\_CM.txt†L53-L59】 That produces **one global draw**, so adoption uncertainty is not represented as a mixture inside the resulting cost distributi bernoulli(p\_hydro)`is a **distribution** (SampleSetDist), and`drawByToggle`uses`SampleSet.map(...)`to apply conditional sampling per sample. That’s exactly what`SampleSet.map\` is for. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/SampleSet))

### **2\) “Hidden scale / utilization embedded in $/kg terms”**

Your v0.1 has:

* `capex_pharma = 5 to 25 // $ per annual kg capacity`【161:0†squiggle\_CM.txt†L111-L116】  
* `fixed_opex = 1 to 6 // $/kg`【161:1†squiggle\_CM.txt†L152-L159】

But without explicit throughput, this implies an unspoken “plant world” (scale \+ uptime). That’s the critique.l.docx†L35-L105】

In v0.2, we exptime`,` output\_kgpy\`

* convert fixed opex from an **annual** amount / scaling exponent back into $/kg  
* divide cst-case 100% utilization”

### **3\) “Probabilities/shares shouldn’t be lognormal”**

In Squiggle, `a to b` is shorthand for **lognormal({p5:a, p95:b})**. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/Dist)) That’s inappropriate for \[0,1\] quantities (can exceed 1), so v0.2 uses **beta()** for probabilities and shares. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Api/Dist))

### **4\) “Correlation structure / conditional probabilities”**

Your v0.1 has a bit of conditional structure (hydro affects both media liters and media price), but otherwise many drivers are effectively independent. The eval doc calls out this simplification as a concern. 【155:5†Cultivated Meat Model Eval.docx†L9-L18】

In v0.2, the **maturity** latent factor ties together:

* adoption probabilities,  
* custom reactor share,  
* WACC,  
* and (if you choose) other “maturity-sensitive” parameters.

That’s an explicit, interpretable alternative to hand-wavy “uniform correlations”.

### **5\) The “75%ard-coded**

Your notebook text claims:

* hydrolysates reduce media costs by \~75% (hard-coded text)  
* food-grade micros reduce micros by \~90% (hard-coded text)

But those numbers are not actually used in the calculation—your costs change because the conditional ranges change. So v0.2 includes `implied_media_reduction` and `implied_comm_micros_reduction` computed from the parameterization itself.

---

## **How to incorporate your “Pivotal Questions” (without me seeing the CSV)**

I **could not retrieve the actual PQ table values** from the CSV you uploaded using the document search tool (it didn’t surface any chunks), so I can’t pre-fill the exact elicited ranges.

But the model is structured so evaluators can paste them into the “PQ inputs” block:

* PQs about **adoption likelihood by 2036** → `p_hydro_base`, `p_foodgrade_base`, `p_recfactors_base`  
* PQs about **scale / commercialization maturity** → `plant_kta`, `maturity`  
* PQs about **utilization** → `uptime`  
* PQs about **financing / risk** → `wacc_base`  
* PQs about **process needs** (media L/kg, micros g/kg, GF needs) → the conditional ranges inside `drawByToggle(...)`

If you want: paste (or screenshot) the relevant rows of the PQ table here, and I’ll rewrite the parameter block to match them 1:1.

---

# **Model 1.5: “Intermediate” approach (same structure, but inputs are selectable)**

If you want something PQ evaluators can use *without editing code*, the cleanest pattern is:

1. Put the **core model** (above) in one file.  
     
2. Build an **input schema** (JSON/YAML/CSV) listing parameters with:  
     
   * name  
   * distribution type (uniform/lognormal/beta)  
   * parameters (p5/p95 or mean/stdev)  
   * notes / evidence links

   

3. A small UI renders each parameter as:  
     
   * slider(s) for mean and uncertainty, or  
   * dropdown of “distribution family”

   

4. The UI compiles those into a Squiggle model string (or runs the model in Python).

Squiggle has tooling intended for running models outside SquiggleHub (runner \+ serialization), which is what you’d want for a dynamic site. ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Internal/Runners))

---

## **Claude Code prompt for building that interactive web UI**

You said “if it works better, provide instructions to Claude Code to build the interface.” Here’s a prompt that usually works well:

Build a Next.js (TypeScript) web app that loads a parameter schema (JSON) describing a cultured meat cost model. The schema includes: parameter id, label, description, distribution family (uniform/lognormal/beta), and parameters (p5/p95 or mean/stdev), plus default values. The UI should render controls for each parameter, show the implied distribution (sparkline / quantiles), and run a Monte Carlo simulation client-side (100k samples). Outputs: unit cost distribution, p5/p50/p95, probability cost \< $10/kg, and a stacked cost breakdown. Provide a way to “lock scenario” toggles (hydrolysates on/off, foodgrade on/off, cheap rec factors on/off) and compare scenario outputs side-by-side. Implement Spearman sensitivity (tornado chart). Export: a downloadable JSON of chosen assumptions and a markdown report of results. Use clean component architecture: ParameterPanel, OutputPanel, ScenarioPanel, SensitivityPanel. Do not hardcode parameter values; everything comes from schema.

If you want this to run **actual Squiggle code** rather than reimplementing Monte Carlo, you’d use the Squiggle runner tooling (JS). ([Squiggle Language](https://www.squiggle-language.com/docs/Internal/Runners))

---

# **Model 2: First‑principles “mechanistic TEA skeleton” (Python)**

DR: next steps to do 

1. Host an explained, visualized, interactive version of the model (get Claude Code to build a Quarto page or something)  
2. Should integrate expandable explanations  
3. Allow users to tweak parameters  
4. Share this with domain experts, including intermediate feedback in the interim   
5. Later, even an ‘ask questions about preferences generate model’ interface    
6. also perhaps some “sensitivity analysis” … either formal mathematical methods, or one driven by the responses of users

This is a **separate model**, not path-dependent on the UC Davis calculator structure. It’s intentionally “engineering-ish” but still simple enough for PQ evaluators.

Core idea:

1. **Biology/process** determines:  
     
   * final cell density (g/L),  
   * cycle time (days),  
   * media turnover factor,  
   * hence **L media per kg product** and **required reactor volume**.

   

2. **Scale \+ uptime** determine annual output.  
     
3. Reactor volume \+ cost per L \+ scaling → **capex** → annualized → $/kg.  
     
4. Then add variable inputs \+ fixed overhead.

You can plug PQ responses in by editing one dictionary (or reading from a CSV).

```py
# cultured_meat_first_principles_model.py
from __future__ import annotations
import math
import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass

Z_5 = -1.6448536269514722
Z_95 =  1.6448536269514722

def sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, p5, p95, n):
    """Lognormal with p5/p95 specified."""
    p5 = np.asarray(p5); p95 = np.asarray(p95)
    mu = (np.log(p5) + np.log(p95)) / 2.0
    sigma = (np.log(p95) - np.log(p5)) / (2.0 * abs(Z_95))
    return np.exp(mu + sigma * rng.standard_normal(n))

def sample_uniform(rng, lo, hi, n):
    return rng.uniform(lo, hi, size=n)

def beta_from_mean_stdev(mean, stdev):
    var = stdev**2
    max_var = mean*(1-mean)
    if var <= 0 or var >= max_var:
        raise ValueError(f"Invalid beta mean/stdev: mean={mean}, stdev={stdev}")
    t = max_var/var - 1.0
    a = mean * t
    b = (1-mean) * t
    return a, b

def sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, mean, stdev, n):
    a, b = beta_from_mean_stdev(mean, stdev)
    return rng.beta(a, b, size=n)

@dataclass
class Params:
    # Scale + operations
    plant_kta_p5: float = 5
    plant_kta_p95: float = 50
    uptime_mean: float = 0.90
    uptime_sd: float = 0.05

    # Maturity
    maturity_mean: float = 0.50
    maturity_sd: float = 0.20

    # Adoption probabilities (base)
    p_hydro_mean: float = 0.60
    p_hydro_sd: float = 0.10
    p_foodgrade_mean: float = 0.65
    p_foodgrade_sd: float = 0.10
    p_recfactors_mean: float = 0.50
    p_recfactors_sd: float = 0.15

    # Process "first principles"
    # Final biomass density at harvest (g/L); drives L/kg ~ 1000/density * turnover
    density_gL_p5: float = 30
    density_gL_p95: float = 200
    cycle_days_p5: float = 0.5
    cycle_days_p95: float = 5.0
    media_turnover_p5: float = 1.0    # 1.0 = batch-like; >1 = perfusion/high replacement
    media_turnover_p95: float = 10.0

    # Media $/L (conditional on hydrolysates)
    media_cost_hydro_p5: float = 0.2
    media_cost_hydro_p95: float = 1.2
    media_cost_nohydro_p5: float = 1.0
    media_cost_nohydro_p95: float = 4.0

    # Commodity micros
    comm_micros_gkg_fg_p5: float = 0.1
    comm_micros_gkg_fg_p95: float = 2.0
    comm_micros_gkg_nofg_p5: float = 1.0
    comm_micros_gkg_nofg_p95: float = 10.0
    comm_micros_price_fg_p5: float = 0.02
    comm_micros_price_fg_p95: float = 2.0
    comm_micros_price_nofg_p5: float = 0.5
    comm_micros_price_nofg_p95: float = 20.0

    # Recombinant factors (growth factors / proteins)
    recfac_gkg_cheap_p5: float = 1e-4
    recfac_gkg_cheap_p95: float = 1e-2
    recfac_gkg_exp_p5: float = 1e-3
    recfac_gkg_exp_p95: float = 2e-1
    recfac_price_cheap_p5: float = 0.05
    recfac_price_cheap_p95: float = 20.0
    recfac_price_exp_p5: float = 5.0
    recfac_price_exp_p95: float = 500.0

    # Energy etc (variable other)
    other_var_p5: float = 0.5
    other_var_p95: float = 5.0

    # Reactor/capex first principles
    reactor_cost_per_L_pharma_p5: float = 50
    reactor_cost_per_L_pharma_p95: float = 500
    custom_reactor_cost_ratio_p5: float = 0.35
    custom_reactor_cost_ratio_p95: float = 0.85
    custom_reactor_share_mean: float = 0.55
    custom_reactor_share_sd: float = 0.15

    capex_scale_exp_p5: float = 0.6
    capex_scale_exp_p95: float = 0.9
    # “Lang factor” for total installed plant vs just bioreactors
    plant_factor_p5: float = 1.5
    plant_factor_p95: float = 3.5

    # Financing
    wacc_p5: float = 0.08
    wacc_p95: float = 0.20
    asset_life_lo: float = 8
    asset_life_hi: float = 20

    # Fixed opex annual (at 20kTA reference), then scale
    fixed_opex_perkg_ref_lo: float = 1.0
    fixed_opex_perkg_ref_hi: float = 6.0
    fixed_opex_scale_exp_lo: float = 0.6
    fixed_opex_scale_exp_hi: float = 1.0

def crf(wacc, n_years):
    return wacc * (1+wacc)**n_years / ((1+wacc)**n_years - 1)

def simulate(n=200_000, seed=1, P: Params = Params()):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)

    # Latent maturity
    maturity = sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.maturity_mean, P.maturity_sd, n)

    # Scale and uptime
    plant_kta = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.plant_kta_p5, P.plant_kta_p95, n)
    uptime = sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.uptime_mean, P.uptime_sd, n)

    output_kgpy = plant_kta * 1e6 * uptime  # kg/year produced

    # Adoption probabilities (maturity-adjusted)
    p_hydro = np.clip(sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.p_hydro_mean, P.p_hydro_sd, n) + 0.25*(maturity-0.5), 0, 1)
    p_foodgrade = np.clip(sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.p_foodgrade_mean, P.p_foodgrade_sd, n) + 0.20*(maturity-0.5), 0, 1)
    p_recf = np.clip(sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.p_recfactors_mean, P.p_recfactors_sd, n) + 0.25*(maturity-0.5), 0, 1)

    is_hydro = rng.random(n) < p_hydro
    is_foodgrade = rng.random(n) < p_foodgrade
    is_recf_cheap = rng.random(n) < p_recf

    # Process intensities (first principles)
    density_gL = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.density_gL_p5, P.density_gL_p95, n)
    cycle_days = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.cycle_days_p5, P.cycle_days_p95, n)
    media_turnover = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.media_turnover_p5, P.media_turnover_p95, n)

    # L/kg is "how many liters of culture volume you need per kg harvested", times media turnover
    # Base batch: L/kg ~ 1000/density(g/L)
    L_per_kg = (1000.0 / density_gL) * media_turnover

    # Media $/L conditional on hydro
    media_cost_L = np.where(
        is_hydro,
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.media_cost_hydro_p5, P.media_cost_hydro_p95, n),
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.media_cost_nohydro_p5, P.media_cost_nohydro_p95, n),
    )
    cost_media = L_per_kg * media_cost_L

    # Commodity micros conditional on food-grade
    g_comm = np.where(
        is_foodgrade,
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.comm_micros_gkg_fg_p5, P.comm_micros_gkg_fg_p95, n),
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.comm_micros_gkg_nofg_p5, P.comm_micros_gkg_nofg_p95, n),
    )
    price_comm = np.where(
        is_foodgrade,
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.comm_micros_price_fg_p5, P.comm_micros_price_fg_p95, n),
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.comm_micros_price_nofg_p5, P.comm_micros_price_nofg_p95, n),
    )
    cost_comm_micros = g_comm * price_comm

    # Recombinant factors conditional on cheap tech
    g_recf = np.where(
        is_recf_cheap,
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.recfac_gkg_cheap_p5, P.recfac_gkg_cheap_p95, n),
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.recfac_gkg_exp_p5, P.recfac_gkg_exp_p95, n),
    )
    price_recf = np.where(
        is_recf_cheap,
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.recfac_price_cheap_p5, P.recfac_price_cheap_p95, n),
        sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.recfac_price_exp_p5, P.recfac_price_exp_p95, n),
    )
    cost_recf = g_recf * price_recf

    other_var = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.other_var_p5, P.other_var_p95, n)
    voc = cost_media + cost_comm_micros + cost_recf + other_var

    # Reactor volume requirement from productivity
    # productivity (kg/L/day) ~ (density_gL/1000)/cycle_days
    prod_kg_L_day = (density_gL / 1000.0) / cycle_days
    total_working_volume_L = output_kgpy / (prod_kg_L_day * 365.0)

    # Reactor CAPEX: cost/L with custom share
    reactor_cost_L_pharma = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.reactor_cost_per_L_pharma_p5, P.reactor_cost_per_L_pharma_p95, n)
    custom_ratio = sample_uniform(rng, P.custom_reactor_cost_ratio_p5, P.custom_reactor_cost_ratio_p95, n)
    custom_share = np.clip(sample_beta_mean_stdev(rng, P.custom_reactor_share_mean, P.custom_reactor_share_sd, n) + 0.30*(maturity-0.5), 0, 1)

    reactor_cost_L_avg = reactor_cost_L_pharma * (custom_share*custom_ratio + (1-custom_share))

    # CAPEX scaling exponent and plant factor
    capex_s = sample_uniform(rng, P.capex_scale_exp_p5, P.capex_scale_exp_p95, n)
    plant_factor = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.plant_factor_p5, P.plant_factor_p95, n)

    # Approx total installed CAPEX: (cost/L)*V * scaling adjustment * plant factor
    # scaling: K ~ V^s so relative to linear K_lin ~ V, apply V^(s-1)
    V_ref = 1e6  # 1 million liters as arbitrary reference for numeric stability
    capex_total = (reactor_cost_L_avg * total_working_volume_L) * ((total_working_volume_L / V_ref) ** (capex_s - 1)) * plant_factor

    # Finance
    wacc = sample_lognormal_p5_p95(rng, P.wacc_p5, P.wacc_p95, n) - 0.03*(maturity-0.5)
    wacc = np.clip(wacc, 0.03, None)
    asset_life = sample_uniform(rng, P.asset_life_lo, P.asset_life_hi, n)
    crf_val = crf(wacc, asset_life)

    capex_perkg = (capex_total * crf_val) / output_kgpy

    # Fixed opex: define annual at 20kTA reference, scale with output
    ref_output = 20e6 * 0.9
    fixed_perkg_ref = sample_uniform(rng, P.fixed_opex_perkg_ref_lo, P.fixed_opex_perkg_ref_hi, n)
    fixed_annual_ref = fixed_perkg_ref * ref_output
    fixed_scale = sample_uniform(rng, P.fixed_opex_scale_exp_lo, P.fixed_opex_scale_exp_hi, n)
    fixed_annual = fixed_annual_ref * ((output_kgpy / ref_output) ** fixed_scale)
    fixed_perkg = fixed_annual / output_kgpy

    unit_cost = voc + capex_perkg + fixed_perkg

    # Summaries
    def q(x, p): return float(np.quantile(x, p))
    out = {
        "p5": q(unit_cost, 0.05),
        "p50": q(unit_cost, 0.50),
        "p95": q(unit_cost, 0.95),
        "mean": float(np.mean(unit_cost)),
        "prob_lt_10": float(np.mean(unit_cost < 10)),
        "prob_lt_25": float(np.mean(unit_cost < 25)),
    }
    return out

if __name__ == "__main__":
    res = simulate()
    print(res)
```

### **Why this “first principles” model is useful for PQ evaluators**

It makes a few *structural* truths obvious:

* If you believe **high achievable density** and **short cycle times**, then:  
    
  * required reactor volume drops,  
  * CAPEX per kg drops,  
  * media per kg drops (in batch-like assumptions).


* If you believe production is **perfusion‑heavy** with high turnover, you can represent that as `media_turnover`, and media costs may explode unless media $/L is low.  
    
* It cleanly separates:  
    
  * **biological/process** drivers (density, cycle, turnover),  
  * **industrialization** drivers (custom reactors cost/L, scaling exponents),  
  * **finance** (WACC/CRF).

That’s exactly the 3‑bucket breakdown you and the critique have been orbiting around—just implemented from process constraints rather than assumed $/kg blocks.

---

# **Main ways you can still improve either model**

Even with v0.2, the biggest missing realism knobs are:

1. **Batch failure / contamination risk** Add a yield loss factor or “effective uptime” distribution that depends on maturity and scale.  
     
2. **Learning curves / time path** Right now it’s a 2036 snapshot. A simple learning curve over time (capex and media cost trending down) would be more realistic.  
     
3. **Downstream processing / texturization / scaffolds** Whole-cut products add steps that can dominate.  
     
4. **Geography & input price heterogeneity** Electricity, labor, regulatory costs.  
     
5. **Endogenous scale** Make plant\_kta depend on expected margin / financing feasibility.

---

If you paste your **PQ table values** (even just the rows you think map to hydro/micros/GFs/scale/uptime/WACC), I’ll do a clean “drop‑in” rewrite of the v0.2 **PQ input block** so it matches your exact elicitation structure and naming.

# **New Model Testing**

# **Selective Testing of the New Model**

### **Numerical Stability Tests**

#### **Baseline**

| Metric | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| 5th Percentile (p5) | $14.61 |
| 50th Percentile (p50) | $56.18 |
| 95th Percentile (p95) | $311.36 |
| Mean Cost | $97.50 |
| Probability \< $10 | 1.16% |
| Probability \< $25 | 18.20% |

As expected based on the use of lognormal and beta distributions, the right skew of synthetic data distributions permeate to model output with typical effects: (1) mean \> median, (2) p5 and p95 not symmetrical around the mean.

#### **Robustness against different random seeds**

| Seed | p5 | p50 | p95 | Mean | Probability \< $10 | Probability \< $25 |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | 14.606 | 56.176 | 311.360 | 97.502 | 0.01156 | 0.18198 |
| 2 | 14.669 | 56.295 | 311.354 | 97.701 | 0.01177 | 0.18089 |
| 3 | 14.709 | 56.000 | 308.810 | 96.770 | 0.01125 | 0.18191 |
| 4 | 14.758 | 56.001 | 312.726 | 97.512 | 0.01111 | 0.18139 |
| 5 | 14.691 | 56.121 | 309.545 | 97.274 | 0.01098 | 0.18175 |
| 6 | 14.711 | 56.028 | 308.862 | 97.233 | 0.01146 | 0.18233 |
| 7 | 14.673 | 56.035 | 310.438 | 97.283 | 0.01135 | 0.18259 |
| 8 | 14.582 | 56.131 | 308.865 | 97.226 | 0.01173 | 0.18291 |
| 9 | 14.701 | 56.485 | 312.916 | 98.166 | 0.01121 | 0.18121 |
| 10 | 14.683 | 56.166 | 308.387 | 97.635 | 0.01143 | 0.18087 |

Using different values of random seeds varies model output by no more than 1% — deemed appropriate.

#### **Robustness against sample size**

| Sample Size | p5 | p50 | p95 | Mean | Probability \< $10 | Probability \< $25 |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 100 | 13.129 | 59.450 | 255.045 | 85.729 | 0.01000 | 0.20000 |
| 1’000 | 15.198 | 55.464 | 311.802 | 99.171 | 0.01100 | 0.18200 |
| 10’000 | 14.757 | 56.197 | 308.883 | 97.262 | 0.01160 | 0.19050 |
| 100’000 | 14.770 | 56.285 | 309.976 | 97.460 | 0.01108 | 0.18126 |

Heavily skewed distributions usually require more data points to converge to their “true” parameters — this is evident when comparing the first row with the rest of the table above, where model output deviates significantly from those simulated using larger sample sizes. The default sample size used in *´simulate´* method *´n=200\_000´* reduces noisiness of obtained results.

### **Sensitivity Tests**

#### **Correlation Structure**

The test calculates pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients between all variables encoded in the program as draws from a predefined distribution, and between these variables and the model output (´unit\_cost´).  
![][image2]  
As is evident in the heatmap above, the great majority of variables show no relation — coefficients close to zero. This underscores the underlying assumption of independence that is implicit in the model. Notable exceptions are seen among those variables where dependence has been explicitly introduced — e.g., ´maturity´ vs ´p\_hydro´, ´p\_foodgrade´ and ´p\_recf´ — and where the modelled cost components directly influence per-unit price — e.g., ´unit\_cost´ vs ´media\_turnover´ and ´media\_cost\_L´.

#### **Impact of Economic Regime**

The improved model, much like the original, blends two economic regimes where inputs to production are orders of magnitude larger under one compared to the other. This test separates the outputs into two distinct regimes defined by hardcoding the ´p\_hydro´, ´p\_foodgrade´ and ´p\_recf´ variables to 1 (cheap regime) or 0 (expensive).

**Cheap Inputs Regime:**

* 5th Percentile (p5): $10.98  
* 50th Percentile (p50): $30.87  
* 95th Percentile (p95): $128.09  
* Mean Cost: $45.81  
* Probability of Cost Less Than $10: 3.41%  
* Probability of Cost Less Than $25: 38.57%

**Expensive Inputs Regime:**

* 5th Percentile (p5): $36.52  
* 50th Percentile (p50): $121.04  
* 95th Percentile (p95): $489.54  
* Mean Cost: $176.22  
* Probability of Cost Less Than $10: 0.0065%  
* Probability of Cost Less Than $25: 1.24%

These results clearly illustrate the impact of input costs on the overall cost distribution of producing cultured meat. In the cheap inputs regime, the costs are significantly lower across all percentiles, and there is a much higher probability of the cost being less than $10 or $25. In contrast, the expensive inputs regime results in much higher costs and a significantly lower probability of the cost being below these thresholds. This fully aligns with underlying intuition.
